tagged_story_for_tts:
Shadows with personalities. Fear shaped into stories.

The phrase "monsters under the bed" isn’t just a saying—it’s a blueprint. Children’s brains are like overactive security systems: they scan for threats, but with limited data, they fill gaps with imagination. A rustling curtain becomes a lurking creature; a creaking floorboard, a stalking beast. This isn’t random. Studies show 70% of 4–6-year-olds invent imaginary beings, often scary ones. Why? Because fear is easier to handle when it has a face.

A monster is a problem with rules. "It hates light" teaches a child to leave the lamp on. "It freezes if you stare" turns terror into a game. Psychologists call this mastery through play—like practicing how to outsmart a lion by first drawing one. Even the shape of these monsters follows logic: spiky teeth (danger), huge eyes (being watched), or too many limbs (unpredictability). They’re not just scary; they’re useful scarecrows for the unknown.

Three related subjects are fear as a teacher, how folklore tames chaos, and why adults stop seeing monsters.
